title: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference for a Generic Function with Union Types

statement.markdown: Write a function that takes a value and a type guard function, and returns the result of applying the type guard function to the value. The type guard function should take a value and return a boolean indicating whether the value is of a particular type. The function should work with union types, i.e., types that can be one of multiple types. For example, if the type guard function returns true for values of type A and B, the function should return the result of the type guard function applied to the value, with the type inferred as A or B. However, the current implementation has a logical bug that causes incorrect type inference for union types.
statement.inputFormat: The function takes two arguments: a value (of any type) and a type guard function (that takes a value and returns a boolean).
statement.outputFormat: The function returns the result of applying the type guard function to the value.
statement.notes: The function should work correctly with union types, i.e., types that can be one of multiple types.

examples.explanation: The type guard function returns true for even numbers, so the result is true.
The type guard function returns false for 10, so the result is false.
The type guard function returns false for numbers, so the result is false.

hints.text: Use a generic type parameter to represent the type of the value and the type guard function. Use the type guard function to narrow the type of the value to a union type.

editorial.title: Correcting the Type Inference for a Generic Function with Union Types
editorial.intuition: The type guard function should be used to narrow the type of the value to a union type. This can be achieved by using a generic type parameter to represent the type of the value and the type guard function.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible solution is to use a switch statement to check the type of the value and apply the type guard function accordingly.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use the type guard function to narrow the type of the value to a union type.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a generic type parameter to represent the type of the value and the type guard function, and use the type guard function to narrow the type of the value to a union type.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correct solution can be proven by showing that it satisfies the type safety properties of the language.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the test cases can be used to verify that the solution works correctly.
commonMistakes: Incorrect type inference for union types
Not using a generic type parameter to represent the type of the value and the type guard function

functionSignature.javascript: function applyTypeGuard(value: any, typeGuard: (x: any) => boolean): any
